full_context: The rated current of the circuit breaker in the output circuit of the charger should be selected according to the rated output current of the charger, using: I_n ≥ K_k I_m. This formula is used to determine the rated current of the charger output-circuit breaker in a DC power supply system, ensuring it meets load requirements while considering the reliability factor.

variable_definitions.name: Circuit breaker rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated current of the circuit protective device

variable_definitions.name: Reliability factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety factor considering calculation error and reliability

variable_definitions.name: Charger rated output current
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum continuous operating current output by the charger

potential_questions: Given charger rated output current I_m=100A, find the minimum circuit breaker rated current I_n
task_description: 1. Obtain charger rated output current \(I_m\). 2. Select reliability factor \(K_k=1.2\). 3. Compute \(I_n=K_k\times I_m\). 4. Round up to the nearest standard circuit breaker rated current.

full_context: The rated current of the circuit breaker in the closing circuit of an electromagnetic operating mechanism for a high-voltage circuit breaker is calculated as: I_n ≥ K_c2 I_c1. This formula is used to determine the rated current of the closing-circuit breaker, ensuring reliable operation under the closing inrush/impact current.

variable_definitions.name: Circuit breaker rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated current of the protective device in the closing circuit

variable_definitions.name: Coordination factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor accounting for characteristics of closing current impact

variable_definitions.name: Closing current
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated closing current of the electromagnetic operating mechanism of the high-voltage circuit breaker

potential_questions: Given closing current I_c1=200A, find circuit breaker rated current I_n
task_description: 1. Obtain closing current \(I_{c1}\). 2. Select coordination factor \(K_{c2}=0.3\). 3. Compute \(I_n=K_{c2}\times I_{c1}\). 4. Round up to the nearest standard circuit breaker rated current.

full_context: Battery quantity calculation formula: n = 1.05 U_n / U_f. This formula is used to determine the number of batteries required to meet the DC bus float-charging voltage requirement, where U_n is the nominal system voltage and U_f is the float-charging voltage of a single battery cell.

variable_definitions.name: Number of batteries
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of individual cells in the battery string

variable_definitions.name: DC system nominal voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated voltage of the DC power system

variable_definitions.name: Single-cell float voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Operating voltage of a single battery cell under float-charging condition

potential_questions: Given U_n=220V and U_f=2.25V, find number of batteries n
task_description: 1. Obtain nominal system voltage \(U_n\). 2. Obtain single-cell float voltage \(U_f\). 3. Compute \(n=1.05\times U_n/U_f\). 4. Round the result up to an integer.

full_context: Initial shock capacity calculation formula for batteries: C_cho = K_k I_cho / K_cho. This formula is used to verify the battery capability to withstand the initial (1 min) shock load during an accident, ensuring discharge requirements are met.

variable_definitions.name: Calculated initial shock capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Required capacity for the battery to withstand a 1-minute shock load

variable_definitions.name: Reliability factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety margin factor

variable_definitions.name: Initial shock discharge current
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum discharge current during the first 1 minute of an accident

variable_definitions.name: Capacity conversion factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacity conversion value corresponding to the 1-minute discharge rate

potential_questions: Given I_cho=500A and K_cho=0.8h⁻¹, find C_cho
task_description: 1. Obtain initial shock discharge current \(I_{cho}\). 2. Obtain capacity conversion factor \(K_{cho}\). 3. Select reliability factor \(K_k=1.40\). 4. Compute \(C_{cho}=K_k\times I_{cho}/K_{cho}\).

full_context: Cable thermal stability check formula: √t = k × S / I_d. This formula is used to verify the cable’s ability to withstand thermal effects during a short circuit, ensuring the cable will not be damaged within the fault clearing time.

variable_definitions.name: Short-circuit clearing time
variable_definitions.meaning: Duration for which short-circuit current acts

variable_definitions.name: Conductor thermal stability coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Constant related to conductor material (take 143 for copper)

variable_definitions.name: Cable cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Conductor cross-sectional area

variable_definitions.name: Short-circuit current
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum RMS current under short-circuit fault

potential_questions: Given S=150mm², I_d=10kA, k=143, requirement t≤0.1s, check if it is satisfied
task_description: 1. Obtain cable cross-sectional area \(S\). 2. Obtain short-circuit current \(I_d\). 3. Determine thermal stability coefficient \(k\). 4. Compute minimum allowable time \(t_{\min}=(k\times S/I_d)^2\). 5. Compare \(t_{\min}\) with the actual clearing time.

full_context: Slope stability distance calculation formula: a ≥ 3.5b - d/tanβ (strip footing). This formula is used to determine the minimum horizontal distance from the edge of a building foundation to the slope crest, ensuring slope stability does not compromise building safety.

variable_definitions.name: Distance from foundation outer edge to slope crest
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety distance to prevent slope instability from affecting the building

variable_definitions.name: Foundation base width
variable_definitions.meaning: Width of the strip footing perpendicular to the slope crest edge

variable_definitions.name: Foundation embedment depth
variable_definitions.meaning: Vertical distance from the foundation base to the ground surface

variable_definitions.name: Slope angle
variable_definitions.meaning: Angle between the slope and the horizontal plane

potential_questions: Given b=1.5m, d=2m, β=45°, find the minimum safe distance a
task_description: 1. Obtain foundation base width \(b\). 2. Obtain foundation embedment depth \(d\). 3. Obtain slope angle \(\beta\). 4. Compute \(a=3.5\times b-d/\tan(\beta)\). 5. Verify \(a\ge 2.5\,\mathrm{m}\).

full_context: The station service low-voltage system should adopt a three-phase four-wire system with the neutral point solidly grounded. The system rated voltage is 380V/220V.

variable_definitions.name: System rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal voltage value of the low-voltage station service system

potential_questions: Given a substation station-service system line voltage is 380V, find the phase voltage
task_description: 1. Confirm the system uses a three-phase four-wire system. 2. Identify line voltage as \(U_{LL}=380\,\mathrm{V}\). 3. For a three-phase four-wire system, phase voltage \(U_{ph}=U_{LL}/\sqrt{3}\). 4. Calculate phase voltage as \(U_{ph}\approx 220\,\mathrm{V}\).

full_context: The accident outage time of a substation should be calculated as 2h.

variable_definitions.name: Accident outage time
variable_definitions.meaning: Duration for which the DC system is designed to sustain power supply

potential_questions: A substation battery capacity is 300Ah and load current is 10A; verify whether it meets the 2h supply requirement
task_description: 1. Obtain battery capacity \(C\) (Ah). 2. Determine accident load current \(I\) (A). 3. Compute actual supply time \(T=C/I\). 4. Verify \(T\ge 2\,\mathrm{h}\).

full_context: For a system with neutral point not solidly grounded, the secondary winding of a star-connected voltage transformer should preferably use neutral-point grounding, and the rated voltage of the residual winding should be 100V/√3. For a solidly grounded neutral system, the rated voltage of the VT residual winding should be 100V.

variable_definitions.name: Rated voltage of residual winding
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal output voltage of the VT open-delta (broken-delta) winding

potential_questions: For a 110kV solidly grounded neutral system, find the rated voltage of the VT residual winding
task_description: 1. Confirm the system neutral grounding method. 2. For a solidly grounded system, select \(U_{\text{residual}}=100\,\mathrm{V}\). 3. For a non-solidly grounded system, select \(U_{\text{residual}}=100/\sqrt{3}\,\mathrm{V}\approx 57.7\,\mathrm{V}\).

full_context: The operating current setting of the instantaneous trip unit of the automatic switch on the secondary side of a voltage transformer should be set greater than the maximum load current of the VT secondary circuit.

variable_definitions.name: Instantaneous trip operating current
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum current threshold that causes the automatic switch to trip

variable_definitions.name: Maximum load current
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum possible current in the VT secondary circuit

potential_questions: If the maximum load current of a VT secondary circuit is 5A, determine the automatic switch setting
task_description: 1. Measure or calculate the maximum load current of the secondary circuit \(I_{\text{load-max}}\). 2. Set \(I_{trip}>I_{\text{load-max}}\) (e.g., \(1.2\) times). 3. Output the setting value \(I_{trip}\).

full_context: When selecting UPS rated capacity, in addition to calculating apparent power based on the load, requirements for dynamic (step change of load from 0–100%) voltage and frequency regulation accuracy, as well as influences of temperature variation, battery terminal voltage drop, and design redundancy requirements, should also be considered.

variable_definitions.name: UPS rated capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum UPS apparent power that satisfies all design conditions

variable_definitions.name: Load apparent power
variable_definitions.meaning: Total apparent power demand of all connected equipment

variable_definitions.name: Comprehensive factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety factor including dynamic response/temperature/redundancy, etc. (typically ≥1.1)

potential_questions: A substation monitoring system load apparent power is 10kVA; take k=1.2; find minimum UPS capacity
task_description: 1. Sum apparent power of all loads \(S_{\text{load}}\). 2. Determine comprehensive factor \(k\) (typically 1.1–1.5). 3. Compute \(S_{\text{UPS}}=k\times S_{\text{load}}\). 4. Round up to a standard UPS capacity.

full_context: Panels/cabinets equipped with electronic devices should be provided with a main grounding plate for common zero-potential reference-point (logic) grounding. The cross-sectional area of the copper busbar of the main grounding plate shall not be less than 100mm².

variable_definitions.name: Grounding copper busbar cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum conductor cross-section to ensure grounding performance

potential_questions: A cabinet grounding copper busbar is 10mm×8mm; verify whether it meets the requirement
task_description: 1. Measure busbar cross-section dimensions. 2. Compute actual area \(A=\text{width}\times\text{thickness}\). 3. Verify \(A\ge 100\,\mathrm{mm}^2\).

formula: t_{\text{clearing}} = \begin{cases} 0.08s & \text{500kV near end} \\ 0.10s & \text{500kV remote end} \\ 0.10s & \text{220kV near end} \\ 0.12s & \text{220kV remote end} \end{cases}
full_context: 5.3.1 The line fault clearing time includes the circuit breaker complete opening time and the relay protection operating time. For 500kV lines: 0.08s at the near-fault end and 0.1s at the remote-fault end; for 220kV lines: 0.1s at the near-fault end and 0.12s at the remote-fault end. This is used to determine fault clearing time thresholds for lines of different voltage levels and is a key parameter in power system stability analysis.

variable_definitions.name: Fault clearing time
variable_definitions.meaning: Total time from fault inception to complete circuit breaker opening

variable_definitions.name: Near-fault end
variable_definitions.meaning: Line terminal closer to the fault location

variable_definitions.name: Remote-fault end
variable_definitions.meaning: Line terminal farther from the fault location

potential_questions: Given a 500kV line remote-end fault, find the maximum allowable clearing time
task_description: 1. Identify the faulted line voltage level (500 kV/220 kV). 2. Determine fault location category (near end/remote end). 3. Select the corresponding time value \(t_{\text{clearing}}\) from the specified thresholds. 4. Output the result.

full_context: 5.4.1 The minimum single-phase reclosing time is constrained by arc extinction and insulation recovery: for 220kV lines, or for 330kV lines with length ≤150km, and for 500kV lines with length ≤100km (without shunt reactors), it should be ≥0.5s. This ensures that line insulation has recovered before reclosing, preventing a secondary fault.

variable_definitions.name: Reclosing time
variable_definitions.meaning: Time interval from fault clearing to circuit breaker reclosing

variable_definitions.name: Line length
variable_definitions.meaning: Physical length of the transmission line

potential_questions: A 500kV line is 80km long; find the minimum reclosing time
task_description: 1. Obtain the line voltage level. 2. Measure line length \(L\). 3. Determine whether it meets the short-line condition (500 kV: \(L\le 100\,\mathrm{km}\); 330 kV: \(L\le 150\,\mathrm{km}\)). 4. If yes, output \(t_{\text{reclose}}\ge 0.5\,\mathrm{s}\). 5. Otherwise, determine based on actual tests.

full_context: 5.5.1 The station-control-layer system availability shall not be less than 99.9%. Availability is calculated as: A_available = (total calendar hours of the month - system monthly outage hours)/total calendar hours of the month × 100%. This index evaluates the reliability of the monitoring system; outage time includes hardware failures, software failures, and equivalent time converted from partial functional failures.

variable_definitions.name: System availability
variable_definitions.meaning: Percentage of normal operating time over total time

variable_definitions.name: Monthly outage hours
variable_definitions.meaning: Total time the system is unavailable

variable_definitions.name: Calendar hours
variable_definitions.meaning: Total duration of the statistical period

potential_questions: A system has 720 hours in a month and 2 hours outage; verify whether it meets the requirement
task_description: 1. Record total calendar time \(T_{\text{calendar}}\). 2. Sum system outage time \(T_{\text{outage}}\). 3. Compute \(A_{\text{availability}}=(T_{\text{calendar}}-T_{\text{outage}})/T_{\text{calendar}}\times 100\%\). 4. Check whether it is \(\ge 99.9\%\).

full_context: 5.5.4 Network load rate shall be ≤20% under normal conditions and ≤40% under accident conditions. Load rate reflects network data transmission utilization; excessive load can cause communication delays. These thresholds ensure the monitoring system can still maintain transmission of critical data under accident conditions.

variable_definitions.name: Network load rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual network data throughput to maximum capacity

potential_questions: Network bandwidth is 100Mbps; normal traffic 15Mbps; accident traffic 35Mbps. Verify whether it meets the standard.
task_description: 1. Measure actual network traffic \(B_{\text{actual}}\). 2. Obtain network maximum bandwidth \(B_{\max}\). 3. Compute \(\eta_{\text{network}}=(B_{\text{actual}}/B_{\max})\times 100\%\). 4. Check whether normal condition is \(\le 20\%\). 5. Check whether accident condition is \(\le 40\%\).

full_context: 5.5.13 specifies the electromagnetic immunity test levels for bay-level equipment in 500kV substations: electrostatic discharge shall meet Level 4 (contact discharge 8kV, air discharge 15kV); radiated electromagnetic field shall meet Level 3 (10V/m). This requirement ensures reliable equipment operation in strong electromagnetic environments.

variable_definitions.name: Test level
variable_definitions.meaning: Severity classification of electromagnetic immunity tests

variable_definitions.name: kilovolt
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage unit for electrostatic discharge

variable_definitions.name: volt per meter
variable_definitions.meaning: Electric field strength unit

potential_questions: If a device passes Level 4 ESD test (contact discharge 8kV), is it compliant?
task_description: 1. Identify the test item (ESD/radiated EM field, etc.). 2. Obtain the required level \(\mathrm{Level}\) from the specification. 3. Compare with the immunity test report. 4. Output verification conclusion.

full_context: 5.5.12 The time-synchronization accuracy error of the whole system shall be ≤1ms. This index is achieved via GPS clock synchronization, ensuring event sequence recording (SOE) time resolution ≤2ms. Time synchronization accuracy is a key basis for fault analysis.

variable_definitions.name: Time synchronization error
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum deviation between device clocks and standard time

potential_questions: Given SOE resolution requirement is 2ms in a substation, verify whether the time synchronization system meets the requirement
task_description: 1. Deploy a GPS time synchronization system. 2. Measure clock offsets at all nodes. 3. Record the maximum time deviation \(\Delta t_{\text{sync}}\). 4. Check whether it is \(\le 1\,\mathrm{ms}\).

full_context: 6.3.3 Control levels in descending order are: local, station main control, and remote dispatch center. Only one level of control is allowed at any given time. This defines the control-right switching logic to prevent operational conflicts.

variable_definitions.name: Local control
variable_definitions.meaning: Operating authority executed at the equipment site

variable_definitions.name: Station-control-layer control
variable_definitions.meaning: Operating authority via the monitoring system in the main control room

variable_definitions.name: Remote dispatch control
variable_definitions.meaning: Remote operating authority from the dispatch center

potential_questions: When the station-control layer issues a command, does local control have priority to interrupt?
task_description: 1. Identify current control-right ownership. 2. Receive a new control request. 3. Rank by priority \(\text{local} > \text{station} > \text{remote}\). 4. If the new request has higher priority, switch control right.

full_context: 5.5.3 The mean time between failures (MTBF) of bay-level equipment shall not be less than 30,000 hours. MTBF = total operating time / number of failures. This index measures the reliability of hardware equipment.

variable_definitions.name: Mean time between failures
variable_definitions.meaning: Average continuous normal operating time of equipment

potential_questions: A bay-level device has accumulated 120,000 operating hours with 4 failures; verify whether it meets the requirement
task_description: 1. Record total operating time \(T\). 2. Count number of failures \(N\). 3. Compute \(\mathrm{MTBF}=T/N\). 4. Check whether it is \(\ge 30{,}000\,\mathrm{h}\).

full_context: 3.1.1 Auxiliary power loads in thermal power plants are classified into Class 0 and non-Class 0 according to their importance to personal safety and equipment safety. A Class 0 load outage directly affects personal safety or the safety of major equipment, and is subdivided into Class 0I (AC uninterruptible load), Class 0II (DC essential load), and Class 0III (AC essential load). Non-Class 0 loads are classified into Classes I, II, and III according to process importance.

variable_definitions.name: Class 0 load
variable_definitions.meaning: Load whose outage directly affects personal safety or the safety of major equipment

variable_definitions.name: AC uninterruptible load
variable_definitions.meaning: Load that must be continuously supplied by a UPS

variable_definitions.name: DC essential load
variable_definitions.meaning: Load that must be supplied by a DC power source

variable_definitions.name: AC essential load
variable_definitions.meaning: Load that must be supplied by an AC essential power source

potential_questions: For a 600MW unit, list at least three loads that belong to Class 0I
task_description: 1. Identify loads whose outage affects personal/equipment safety. 2. Distinguish required power source type (AC uninterruptible/DC/AC essential). 3. Classify them as \(\text{Class 0I}\)/\(\text{Class 0II}\)/\(\text{Class 0III}\).

full_context: 3.2.4 Principle for selecting high-voltage auxiliary power supply voltage: for 300MW-class and smaller units, 6kV is recommended; for 600MW-class and larger units, depending on conditions, one level of 6kV, one level of 10kV, or two-level voltages of 10kV/6kV may be adopted. Selection should consider switchgear short-circuit level limits and economy.

variable_definitions.name: High-voltage auxiliary power voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal voltage of the high-voltage electrical system in a thermal power plant

variable_definitions.name: Unit capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated output power of the turbo-generator unit

potential_questions: A 1000MW unit adopts two-level 10kV/6kV for HV auxiliary power; explain its advantages
task_description: 1. Determine the unit capacity class \(P_{\text{unit}}\). 2. Select the recommended voltage level(s) \(U_{\text{HV}}\) based on capacity. 3. Optimize selection considering short-circuit current limits and economy.

full_context: 4.1.1 Auxiliary power load statistics use the conversion-factor method: calculated load S_c = K∑P. K is the conversion factor: take 1.0 for continuously operating equipment; take 0.5 for short-time intermittent equipment; for coal conveying systems, etc., apply corrections according to shift/working schedule.

variable_definitions.name: Calculated load
variable_definitions.meaning: Basis for sizing capacity of the auxiliary power system

variable_definitions.name: Conversion factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor converting according to load operating characteristics

variable_definitions.name: Total installed power
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of rated power of all electrical equipment

potential_questions: A coal crusher P_e=200kW (short-time intermittent) and a circulating water pump P_e=800kW (continuous); calculate total load
task_description: 1. Identify operating characteristics of each device. 2. Determine \(K\) (continuous = 1.0, short-time = 0.5). 3. Compute \(\sum (K\cdot P_e)\). 4. Output \(S_c\).

full_context: 4.2.2 For a low-voltage auxiliary service working transformer with explicit standby, the rated capacity should satisfy: S_e ≥ 1.1 times the calculated load S_c, reserving a 10% margin. For an implicit standby transformer, capacity may be selected according to the calculated load without an additional margin.

variable_definitions.name: Transformer rated capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Designed output capacity of the transformer

variable_definitions.name: Calculated load
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum demand capacity of the system

potential_questions: For a PCC section with calculated load S_c=1250kVA, select the explicit-standby transformer capacity
task_description: 1. Obtain calculated load \(S_c\). 2. Compute \(1.1\times S_c\). 3. Select a standard transformer capacity \(S_e\) such that \(S_e\ge 1.1S_c\).

full_context: 4.5.1 Bus voltage verification requirement during normal motor starting: when the largest-capacity motor starts, the auxiliary bus voltage shall not be lower than 80% of the rated voltage. The motor terminal voltage shall not be lower than 70% of the rated voltage.

variable_definitions.name: Bus voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Instantaneous bus voltage during motor starting

variable_definitions.name: Rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal system voltage

potential_questions: A 6kV bus starts a 1600kW fan; calculate the minimum allowable bus voltage
task_description: 1. Determine system rated voltage \(U_n\). 2. Compute \(0.8\times U_n\). 3. Verify \(U_m\ge 0.8U_n\).

full_context: 4.6.1 Minimum allowable auxiliary bus voltage during grouped motor self-starting: 65–70% for high-voltage bus; 60% for low-voltage bus with separate self-starting; 55% for HV-LV cascaded self-starting. This is used to verify voltage stability during standby power source transfer.

variable_definitions.name: Minimum allowable voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Critical voltage ensuring successful motor self-starting

variable_definitions.name: High-voltage auxiliary bus
variable_definitions.meaning: 6kV/10kV distribution bus

variable_definitions.name: Low-voltage auxiliary bus
variable_definitions.meaning: 380V distribution bus

potential_questions: For grouped self-starting on a 380V bus, find the minimum allowable voltage
task_description: 1. Identify bus voltage level. 2. Determine \(U_{\min}\) from the specified values (HV 65–70% / LV 60%). 3. Verify operating voltage \(\ge U_{\min}\).

full_context: 6.1.1 Short-circuit current calculation for high-voltage auxiliary power systems: the initial RMS value of the periodic component is I'' = source component I''_B + motor feedback component I''_D. I''_D = K_q·D ∑P_e/(√3 U_e η cosφ), where K_q·D is taken as 5.5–6.0.

variable_definitions.name: Periodic component of short-circuit current
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS value of the AC component at the instant of short circuit

variable_definitions.name: Source component
variable_definitions.meaning: Short-circuit current provided by the system/source

variable_definitions.name: Motor feedback component
variable_definitions.meaning: Short-circuit current contributed by rotating loads (motor backfeed)

variable_definitions.name: Feedback factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Multiplier for motor feedback current

variable_definitions.name: Total installed motor power
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of rated power of motors

potential_questions: For a 6kV bus with ∑P_e=12MW, calculate motor feedback current I''_D
task_description: 1. Obtain \(\sum P_e\) and \(U_e\). 2. Determine \(K_q\cdot D\) (take 5.5–6.0). 3. Compute \(I^{\prime\prime}_D=(K_q\cdot D)\times\sum P_e/(\sqrt{3}\,U_e\times 0.8)\). 4. Output \(I^{\prime\prime}_D\).

full_context: Appendix A Design auxiliary power ratio estimation: e = (S_c · cosφ_av)/P_g × 100%. S_c is the calculated auxiliary load at 100% unit output (kVA), cosφ_av is taken as 0.8, and P_g is the generator rated power (kW).

variable_definitions.name: Design auxiliary power ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Proportion of plant self-consumption in generated energy

variable_definitions.name: Calculated load
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum demand capacity of the auxiliary power system

variable_definitions.name: Average power factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Overall system power factor

variable_definitions.name: Generator power
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated output power of the unit

potential_questions: For a 300MW unit with S_c=25MVA, calculate the design auxiliary power ratio
task_description: 1. Obtain \(S_c\) and \(P_g\). 2. Take \(\cos\varphi_{av}=0.8\). 3. Compute \(e=(S_c\times\cos\varphi_{av})/P_g\times 100\%\). 4. Output result \(e\).

full_context:
Load calculation adopts the conversion-factor method, and the required transformer capacity is calculated by:
$$S \geq K_1 \cdot P_1 + P_2 + P_3$$
Where: S is the required service transformer capacity (kVA); K is the conversion factor for power (motor) loads, generally K=0.85; P_1 is the sum of power (motor) loads (kW); P_2 is the sum of electric heating loads (kW); P_3 is the sum of lighting loads (kW). An example of load calculation and capacity selection for a 500kV substation service transformer is given in Appendix B. This formula is used for selecting substation service transformer capacity and must satisfy the calculated load requirement.

variable_definitions.name: Service transformer capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total capacity required for the transformer to carry the loads

variable_definitions.name: Power load conversion factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Conversion factor from power (motor) loads to capacity

variable_definitions.name: Sum of power loads
variable_definitions.meaning: Total power demand of all motor-driven equipment

variable_definitions.name: Sum of electric heating loads
variable_definitions.meaning: Total power demand of all electric heating equipment

variable_definitions.name: Sum of lighting loads
variable_definitions.meaning: Total power demand of all lighting equipment

potential_questions: Given P₁=250kW, P₂=80kW, P₃=50kW, calculate minimum transformer capacity S
task_description: 1. Obtain total power loads \(P_1\). 2. Obtain total electric heating loads \(P_2\). 3. Obtain total lighting loads \(P_3\). 4. Take conversion factor \(K=0.85\). 5. Compute \(K\cdot P_1\). 6. Add \(K\cdot P_1\), \(P_2\), and \(P_3\). 7. The result is the minimum required transformer capacity \(S\).

full_context:
Three-phase short-circuit peak (impact) current calculation:
$$i_{ch} = \sqrt{2} \times k_{ch} \cdot I^{\prime \prime}$$
Where: i_ch is the short-circuit peak (impact) current (kA); k_ch is the impact coefficient of short-circuit current, which can be determined based on the ratio ΣX/ΣR of the circuit; I'' is the initial RMS value of the periodic component of the three-phase short-circuit current (kA). The impact coefficient k_ch is obtained from k_ch = 1 + e^{-0.01/T}, where T_a = L/R is the circuit time constant (s). This formula is used to evaluate the maximum instantaneous current during a short circuit.

variable_definitions.name: Short-circuit peak (impact) current
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum instantaneous current value at short-circuit inception

variable_definitions.name: Impact coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor characterizing the ratio between short-circuit current peak and periodic component

variable_definitions.name: Initial RMS value of periodic component
variable_definitions.meaning: Initial RMS value of the periodic component of short-circuit current

variable_definitions.name: Circuit time constant
variable_definitions.meaning: Electromagnetic transient time constant determined by L/R ratio

potential_questions: Given I''=15kA and circuit X/R=10, find impact current i_ch
task_description: 1. Compute the circuit \(X/R\) ratio. 2. Obtain \(k_{ch}=f(X/R)\) from charts or calculation. 3. Take periodic component \(I^{\prime\prime}\). 4. Use \(\sqrt{2}\approx 1.414\). 5. Compute \(i_{ch}=\sqrt{2}\times k_{ch}\times I^{\prime\prime}\). 6. Output the impact current \(i_{ch}\).

full_context:
Sensitivity check for overcurrent trip unit:
$$\frac{I_d}{I_Z} \geq 1.5$$
Where: I_d is the minimum short-circuit current at the end of the supply circuit (A); I_Z is the trip unit setting current (A). This formula is used to verify the sensitivity of circuit breaker protection to ensure reliable operation during faults. Notes include: when using current-limiting fuses or fuse links with rated current ≤60A, thermal stability check may be omitted; when protected by current-limiting circuit breakers, thermal stability check may be omitted.

variable_definitions.name: Minimum short-circuit current
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum fault current when a short circuit occurs at the end of the protection zone

variable_definitions.name: Trip unit setting current
variable_definitions.meaning: Operating setting value of the circuit breaker overcurrent protection

potential_questions: Given I_Z=200A and end short-circuit current I_d=400A, check whether sensitivity is acceptable
task_description: 1. Obtain minimum end short-circuit current \(I_d\). 2. Obtain trip unit setting \(I_Z\). 3. Compute ratio \(I_d/I_Z\). 4. Check whether the ratio is \(\ge 1.5\). 5. If \(\ge 1.5\), it passes; otherwise adjust the setting.

full_context:
Low-voltage bus voltage calculation:
$$U_m = U_0 - S \cdot Z_b$$
where: $Z_b = R_b \cdot \cos \varphi + X_b \cdot \sin \varphi$. Here U_m is the low-voltage bus voltage (per-unit); U_0 is the no-load voltage on the low-voltage side of the service transformer (per-unit); S is the service load (per-unit); Z_b is the load voltage-drop impedance (per-unit); R_b is transformer resistance (per-unit); X_b is transformer reactance (per-unit); cosφ is the load power factor, taken as 0.8. The formula is used to check bus voltage deviation under various operating conditions.

variable_definitions.name: Bus voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual bus voltage under load

variable_definitions.name: No-load voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Transformer secondary voltage under no-load condition

variable_definitions.name: Load per-unit value
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of current load to base capacity

variable_definitions.name: Load voltage-drop impedance
variable_definitions.meaning: Load-related component of transformer equivalent impedance

variable_definitions.name: Transformer resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent resistance of transformer winding

variable_definitions.name: Transformer reactance
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent reactance of transformer winding

variable_definitions.name: Power factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of active power to apparent power

potential_questions: Given U₀=1.05, S=0.8, Z_b=0.15, calculate U_m
task_description: 1. Obtain no-load voltage \(U_0\). 2. Obtain current load \(S\). 3. Compute impedance \(Z_b=R_b\cos\varphi+X_b\sin\varphi\). 4. Compute voltage-drop term \(S\cdot Z_b\). 5. Compute \(U_m=U_0-S\cdot Z_b\). 6. Output bus voltage \(U_m\).

full_context:
Method for selecting plant cavitation coefficient:
The plant cavitation coefficient \sigma_p should preferably be selected based on the incipient cavitation coefficient \sigma_i. If \sigma_i is not available, \sigma_p may be selected as the critical cavitation coefficient \sigma_c multiplied by a ratio factor K_\sigma, i.e., \sigma_p = K_\sigma · \sigma_c. For clear water conditions, K_\sigma = 1.1–1.6; for silt-laden flow, K_\sigma = 1.3–1.8. This formula is used to determine the anti-cavitation safety margin for a turbine.

variable_definitions.name: Plant cavitation coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum installation coefficient ensuring the turbine does not cavitate

variable_definitions.name: Safety factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety multiplier applied to the critical cavitation coefficient

variable_definitions.name: Critical cavitation coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Cavitation inception coefficient determined by model tests

potential_questions: Given σ_c=0.12 under silt-laden conditions, take K_σ=1.5; find σ_p
task_description: 1. Obtain critical cavitation coefficient \(\sigma_c\). 2. Select \(K_{\sigma}\) based on water quality (clear water 1.1–1.6, turbid/silt-laden 1.3–1.8). 3. Compute \(\sigma_p=K_{\sigma}\times\sigma_c\). 4. Output plant cavitation coefficient \(\sigma_p\).

full_context:
380V short-circuit current periodic component calculation:
$$I'' = \frac{U}{\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{(\Sigma R)^2 + (\Sigma X)^2}}$$
Where: I'' is the initial RMS value of the periodic component of three-phase short-circuit current (kA); U is the line voltage on the transformer low-voltage side, taken as 400V; ΣR is the total resistance per phase of the circuit (mΩ); ΣX is the total reactance per phase of the circuit (mΩ). Calculation principles: (1) calculate based on a single service transformer; (2) include resistance; (3) system impedance is determined by the breaking capacity of the HV-side protective device; (4) motor feedback current is not considered.

variable_definitions.name: Initial periodic component value
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS value of power-frequency current at short-circuit inception

variable_definitions.name: Line voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal system line voltage

variable_definitions.name: Total resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of resistances of elements in the short-circuit loop

variable_definitions.name: Total reactance
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of reactances of elements in the short-circuit loop

potential_questions: Given ΣR=25mΩ and ΣX=40mΩ, calculate I''
task_description: 1. Set system voltage \(U=400\,\mathrm{V}\). 2. Determine total resistance \(\Sigma R\). 3. Determine total reactance \(\Sigma X\). 4. Compute impedance magnitude \(\sqrt{(\Sigma R)^2+(\Sigma X)^2}\). 5. Compute denominator \(\sqrt{3}\times\sqrt{(\Sigma R)^2+(\Sigma X)^2}\). 6. Compute \(I^{\prime\prime}=400/\text{(denominator)}\). 7. Output short-circuit current \(I^{\prime\prime}\).
